title: Spaghetti alla Nerano
subtitle: Spaghetti with fried courgette and provolone
excerpt: Fried courgette rounds tossed with spaghetti in a buttery, cheese-enriched sauce, no tomato, no garlic in the sauce itself, just courgette and provolone or pecorino in starchy pasta water. The Amalfitan summer pasta invented at Maria Grazia's restaurant in Nerano.
sourceNotes: Based on the dish attributed to Maria Grazia's restaurant in Nerano on the Sorrento peninsula, documented in Giorgio Locatelli, 'Made in Italy' (2006) and multiple Italian culinary sources. The original uses provolone del Monaco; pecorino or a combination of pecorino and parmesan is used here as a substitute.

content.text: Spaghetti alla Nerano started at Maria Grazia's restaurant in the small fishing village of Nerano. The village sits on the Sorrento peninsula. The dish dates from sometime in the 1950s. The sauce has no tomato and no raw garlic. Just fried courgette, basil, and a local aged provolone cheese. The cheese melts into the pasta water and butter to form a creamy, pale coating. The dish became known outside Campania partly through Stanley Tucci's TV series on Italian food. Demand for it rose sharply in the early 2020s.

The courgette is fried in olive oil until golden and slightly collapsed, not quickly sautéed. This frying concentrates its sweetness and removes excess water, preventing the sauce from becoming watery. The cheese needs to be grated finely and added off the heat with pasta water, stirred vigorously to create an emulsion.

items.name: Courgette
items.prepNote: sliced into thin rounds, about 4 to 5 mm

items.name: Pecorino
items.prepNote: finely grated; provolone del Monaco is traditional, pecorino is the accessible substitute

items.name: Garlic
items.prepNote: for flavouring the oil only, discarded before serving

content.text: Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over a medium-high heat. If using, add {{garlic}} the garlic clove and fry until golden, then discard. Fry {{courgette}} of courgette rounds in batches until golden on both sides, about 3 minutes per batch. Set aside. They should be properly coloured, not just softened.
Cook pasta and emulsify the sauce

Cook {{spaghetti}} of spaghetti in well-salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve a large mug of pasta water. Return the courgette to the pan over a low heat with {{unsalted-butter}} of butter. Add the drained pasta and half the pasta water. Toss vigorously. Remove from the heat. Add {{pecorino}} pecorino and {{parmesan}} parmesan in two additions, tossing constantly and adding pasta water a splash at a time until the sauce is creamy and glossy. Add {{basil}} basil leaves and {{black-pepper-ground}} of black pepper. Serve immediately.

items.fix: Remove from the heat before adding the cheese. Toss constantly and add pasta water gradually, the starch in the water is what creates the creamy emulsion.
items.cause: Cheese added while the pan is too hot

items.fix: The courgette must be golden, not just softened. Properly fried courgette has lost most of its moisture and will not release water into the sauce.
items.cause: Courgette not properly fried, still releasing water

content.text: Nerano is a village of a few hundred people on the tip of the Sorrento peninsula. You can reach it by boat from Positano. Maria Grazia's restaurant, which still runs, is credited with creating this dish in the 1950s. They used the courgette that grew well in the area and the local provolone del Monaco, a semi-hard cow's milk cheese made in the mountains above Sorrento. The dish stayed a local secret for decades. Word spread along the tourist routes of the Amalfi coast and eventually reached cooking programmes and food media.
